questions.prompt: Safe default state library for a new app in 2026?

choices.text: Zustand. ~1KB, hooks-first (no Provider), selectors give component-level subscription (avoiding context's all-consumers-re-render trap), TypeScript-friendly, minimal boilerplate, accessible outside React via `getState()`, middleware support (persistence, devtools, immer). For small-to-medium apps, Zustand produces simpler code than Redux Toolkit with most of the benefits. Reach for Jotai when you want atomic granular subscriptions and lots of derived state; for RTK when you're on a large team needing strict patterns, the best devtools, or migrating from legacy Redux.

questions.explanation: Zustand: minimal, selectors solve context re-render problem, safe default for most new apps.

questions.prompt: Team picks Redux Toolkit. Best practice for server data (posts, users, products)?

choices.text: Plain Redux slices with manual loading flags.

Use RTK Query — Redux Toolkit's built-in server-state library. createApi defines endpoints with `query` and `mutation` builders; auto-generated hooks (`useGetPostsQuery`, `useAddPostMutation`) handle caching, deduplication, automatic refetching, optimistic updates, and cache tag invalidation. Same primitives as TanStack Query but integrated with Redux devtools. Don't write manual loading/error/data reducers — that's the pre-2020 pattern that taught everyone server state needs its own tool. Keep regular Redux slices for genuine client state (theme, current user, UI flags); RTK Query owns server data.
Store all data manually in slices.

questions.explanation: RTK Query for server data; regular slices for client state. Don't reimplement caching manually.
